role_summary: Arkansas civil rights worker; voter-registration organizer in West Helena and Phillips County, Arkansas during the 1960s; collaborator with SNCC field secretaries Myrtle Glascoe (catalog entry 137), Bill Hansen, and Howard Himmelbaum (her first husband); poll-tax-petition organizer and Arkansas state capitol protester; later member of Black United Youth in Little Rock; subject of an employment-discrimination lawsuit against her employer.
ai_reading: Young's top semantic neighbor is James Oscar Jones (entry 57) at a cosine count of 138; the broader cluster includes Charles McLaurin (entry 17), Juadine Henderson (entry 68), and Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones (entry 92). The novel observation her page surfaces is the Arkansas-Phillips-County-SNCC-cohort signature: Young's organizing work in West Helena and Phillips County overlapped with Myrtle Glascoe's SNCC field assignment there in the mid-1960s (the same Phillips County the Glascoe catalog entry documents); the embedding catches that shared Arkansas-Phillips-County register but pairs Young more closely with Mississippi-rural-organizing voices, suggesting the Arkansas Delta and Mississippi Delta organizing tradition reads as a single coherent vector signature across state lines.
biographical_paragraph: While Young's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), her external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid for entry 13 [src: 1]. Carrie Young (born Carrie Lamar) was born in 1948 in Barton, Arkansas; she grew up on a family farm and moved with her family to West Helena, Arkansas, in Phillips County, where she met SNCC organizers including Myrtle Glascoe (catalog entry 137), Bill Hansen, and Howard Himmelbaum [src: 1]. She worked alongside them on voter-registration drives, gathering signatures for the petition to overturn the Arkansas poll tax, and protested at the Arkansas State Capitol across the mid-to-late 1960s. She married Howard Himmelbaum in 1967 (their marriage anchored the catalog's clearest Arkansas inter-racial-organizer couple), and later Carl E. Young. She filed a successful employment-discrimination lawsuit against her employer. She attended the Opportunity Industrialization Center and the University of Arkansas-Little Rock, worked across a career in keypunch operating, accounting clerking, publication printing, and data entry / administrative work, and continued community organizing through the group Black United Youth in Little Rock [src: 1].

sources.title: Carrie Lamar Young oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)
